annotations.label: Graph theory

anchor.snippet: Graph theory is a branch of mathematics that studies graphs

annotations.note: A meta-description of the mathematical field, not a formalizable statement.

annotations.label: Graph

anchor.snippet: a graph is a structure consisting of vertices (also called nodes or points) and edges

annotations.note: Undirected graphs are formalized as SimpleGraph (simple) and Graph (general multigraph in Mathlib.Combinatorics.Graph.Basic).

annotations.label: Directed graph

anchor.snippet: A directed graph is a graph where each edge has an assignment direction known as orientation

annotations.note: Quiver formalizes directed graphs (as a directed multigraph, generalizing simple digraphs).

annotations.label: Mixed graph

anchor.snippet: A mixed graph can have edges that may be directed, and some may be undirected

annotations.note: No structure mixing directed and undirected edges exists in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Multigraph

anchor.snippet: A multigraph allows many edges to have the same pair of endpoints

annotations.note: Graph α β models multigraphs with vertex/edge sets and incidence data allowing parallel edges and loops.

annotations.label: Weight graph

anchor.snippet: Such a graph is called a weight graph

annotations.note: No edge-weighted graph structure is defined in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Topological graph theory

anchor.snippet: Topological graph theory deals with the study of graphs as topological spaces

annotations.note: Graph embeddings into surfaces and planarity are absent from Mathlib.

annotations.label: Embedding of a graph in a surface

anchor.snippet: The embedding of a graph in a surface is the representation of a graph

annotations.note: No notion of embedding a graph into a topological surface exists in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Linkless embedding

anchor.snippet: The graph embedding can be generalized into the linkless embedding, whereby no two cycles of the graph are linked

annotations.note: Linkless/spatial embeddings are not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Graph minor

anchor.snippet: The graph is said to be minor if it can be formed from another graph by deleting vertices and edges

annotations.note: Mathlib has subgraph containment (IsContained) but no minor/edge-contraction order.

annotations.label: Wagner's theorem

anchor.snippet: Wagner's theorem , stating that a finite graph is planar if and only if its minor includes neither the complete graph on five vertices

annotations.note: Planarity and forbidden-minor characterizations are not in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Robertson–Seymour theorem

anchor.snippet: the Robertson–Seymour theorem , implying the existence of forbidden minor for every property of graphs

annotations.note: The graph-minor well-quasi-ordering theorem is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Crossing number

anchor.snippet: The crossing number tells the minimum number of crossing edges of a graph

annotations.note: Crossing numbers require graph drawings, which are absent from Mathlib.

annotations.label: Graph coloring

anchor.snippet: A graph coloring is a methodical assignment of labelling the elements of a graph

annotations.note: Proper vertex colorings are formalized as SimpleGraph.Coloring with Colorable and chromaticNumber.

annotations.label: Four-color theorem

anchor.snippet: Four-color theorem stated that no more than four colors are required to color the regions of any map

annotations.note: The four-color theorem is not formalized in Mathlib (it requires planarity).

annotations.label: Voltage graph

anchor.snippet: A voltage graph is a directed graph whose edges are labelled invertibly by elements of a group

annotations.note: Voltage/gain graphs are not defined in Mathlib (though Cayley graphs exist).

annotations.label: Algebraic graph theory

anchor.snippet: Algebraic graph theory is the study of graph theory that involves major branches of algebra

annotations.note: Algebraic tools like adjacency/Laplacian matrices and Cayley graphs exist, but there is no unified development under this name.

annotations.label: Spectral graph theory

anchor.snippet: A study of graph theory using linear algebra is called spectral graph theory

annotations.note: Adjacency/Laplacian matrices and some spectral results (positive semidefiniteness, connected-component kernel dimension) are formalized, but not a general spectral theory.

annotations.label: Frucht's theorem

anchor.snippet: Frucht's theorem states that every finite group is the group of symmetries of a finite undirected graph

annotations.note: Frucht's theorem realizing every finite group as a graph automorphism group is not in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Graph invariant

anchor.snippet: A graph invariant is a property of graphs that depends only on the abstract structure

annotations.note: No general notion of graph invariant (isomorphism-invariant property) is defined in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Chromatic polynomial

anchor.snippet: A chromatic polynomial is a polynomial that counts the number of graph colorings as a function of the number of colors

annotations.note: The chromatic polynomial is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Tutte polynomial

anchor.snippet: The Tutte polynomial is a two-variable polynomial on graph connectivity

annotations.note: The Tutte polynomial is absent (the file Tutte.lean concerns Tutte's matching theorem, not the polynomial).

annotations.label: Geometric graph theory

anchor.snippet: Geometric graph theory focuses on combinatorial and geometric properties of a graph

annotations.note: Geometric graph families (other than unit-distance graphs) and this subfield are not developed in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Planar straight-line graph

anchor.snippet: is called planar straight-line graph

annotations.note: Planar straight-line drawings are not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Fáry's theorem

anchor.snippet: Any planar graph can be represented as a planar straight-line graph by Fáry's theorem

annotations.note: Fáry's theorem on straight-line planar drawings is not in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Euclidean graph

anchor.snippet: The Euclidean graph allows its edges to have the length of the Euclidean distance between its endpoints

annotations.note: Euclidean (geometrically embedded weighted) graphs are not defined in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Visibility graph

anchor.snippet: A visibility graph is a graph whose vertices and edges are the point locations and visible connections

annotations.note: Visibility graphs are not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Polyhedral graph

anchor.snippet: Polyhedral graph is an undirected graph that forms the vertices and edges of a three-dimensional convex polyhedron

annotations.note: Polyhedral graphs are not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Steinitz's theorem

anchor.snippet: Steinitz's theorem , stating that every convex polyhedron is 3-vertex connected planar graph

annotations.note: Steinitz's theorem characterizing polyhedral graphs is not in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Intersection graph

anchor.snippet: An intersection graph is a graph in which each vertex is associated with a set

annotations.note: Intersection graphs of set families are not defined in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Interval graph

anchor.snippet: the intersection graph of line segments in one dimension is an interval graph

annotations.note: Interval graphs are not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Unit disk graph

anchor.snippet: The intersection graph of unit disks in the plane is a unit disk graph

annotations.note: Unit disk graphs are not defined (Mathlib's UnitDistance graphs are a different family).

annotations.label: Coin graph

anchor.snippet: The intersection of a circle packing is a coin graph

annotations.note: Coin/circle-packing graphs are not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Koebe–Andreev–Thurston theorem

anchor.snippet: by Koebe–Andreev–Thurston theorem, the intersection graphs of non-crossing circles are exactly the planar graphs

annotations.note: The circle packing theorem is not in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Scheinerman's theorem

anchor.snippet: Scheinerman's theorem states that every planar graph can be represented as the intersection graph of line segments in the plane

annotations.note: Scheinerman's theorem is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Levi graph

anchor.snippet: The Levi graph is a bipartite graph that associates to the incidence structure

annotations.note: Levi (incidence) graphs are not defined in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Graph drawing

anchor.snippet: another subarea of graph theory that is known as graph drawing

annotations.note: Graph drawing in the plane is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Extremal graph theory

anchor.snippet: Extremal graph theory is a branch of mathematics at the intersection of extremal combinatorics and graph theory

annotations.note: Core extremal machinery (extremalNumber, Turán's theorem, Szemerédi regularity) is formalized, but not the field as a whole.

annotations.label: Mantel's theorem

anchor.snippet: Mantel's theorem on the extremal number of a triangle-free graph

annotations.note: Mantel's theorem is the triangle (r=2) case of the formalized Turán theorem.

annotations.label: Turán's theorem

anchor.snippet: Turán's theorem extended Mantel's theorem for any undirected graph that does not have a complete subgraph of a given size

annotations.note: Turán's theorem is formalized in full as an isomorphism characterization of extremal Kr+1-free graphs.

annotations.label: Erdős–Stone theorem

anchor.snippet: Turán's theorem is generalized by Erdős–Stone theorem

annotations.note: Turán density infrastructure exists, but the Erdős–Stone asymptotic formula itself is not proved.

annotations.label: Forbidden subgraph problem

anchor.snippet: The forbidden subgraphs problem suggests finding the extremal number of a graph

annotations.note: The extremal number ex(n,H) is defined, but the forbidden-subgraph problem itself is an open research program.

annotations.label: Homomorphism density

anchor.snippet: A homomorphism density is a parameter that involves the graph homomorphism

annotations.note: Graph homomorphisms are formalized, but the homomorphism density parameter t(H,G) is not defined.

annotations.label: Szemerédi's regularity lemma

anchor.snippet: Szemerédi's regularity lemma states that a graph can be partitioned into a bounded number of parts

annotations.note: Szemerédi's regularity lemma is formalized as szemeredi_regularity giving an ε-uniform equipartition.

annotations.label: Theory of random graph

anchor.snippet: The theory of random graph focuses on graphs using probabilistic method

annotations.note: The binomial random graph model G(V,p) is constructed, but broader random graph theory is not developed.

annotations.label: Tree

anchor.snippet: A tree is an undirected graph where every pair of vertices is connected by exactly one path

annotations.note: Trees are formalized as SimpleGraph.IsTree (connected and acyclic).

annotations.label: Random tree

anchor.snippet: a random tree is a tree that is formed by a stochastic process

annotations.note: Random trees are not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Uniform spanning tree

anchor.snippet: Uniform spanning tree is a spanning tree of a given graph in which each different tree is equally likely to be selected

annotations.note: Uniform spanning trees (and spanning trees as a counted object) are not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Branching process

anchor.snippet: Branching process , a model of a population in which each individual has a random number of children

annotations.note: Galton–Watson branching processes are not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Brownian tree

anchor.snippet: Brownian tree , a fractal tree structure created by diffusion-limited aggregation processes

annotations.note: Brownian trees are not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Random binary tree

anchor.snippet: Random binary tree is a binary tree selected at random from some probability distribution on binary trees

annotations.note: Random binary trees are not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Random forest

anchor.snippet: Random forest , a machine-learning classifier based on choosing random subsets of variables for each tree

annotations.note: Random forests (a machine-learning method) are not in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Random minimal spanning tree

anchor.snippet: Random minimal spanning tree is a spanning tree of a graph formed by choosing random edge weights

annotations.note: Random minimal spanning trees are not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Random recursive tree

anchor.snippet: Random recursive tree , increasingly labelled trees, which can be generated using a simple stochastic growth rule

annotations.note: Random recursive trees are not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Rapidly exploring random tree

anchor.snippet: Rapidly exploring random tree , a fractal space-filling pattern used as a data structure for searching high-dimensional spaces

annotations.note: RRT data structures are not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Treap

anchor.snippet: Treap or randomized binary search tree, a data structure that uses random choices to simulate a random binary tree

annotations.note: Treaps (randomized BSTs) are not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Network

anchor.snippet: the term network is sometimes defined to mean a graph in which attributes

annotations.note: Networks (attributed/flow graphs) are not formalized as such in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Weighted graph

anchor.snippet: Graphs with weights, or weighted graphs , are used to represent structures in which pairwise connections have some numerical values

annotations.note: No weighted graph structure (edge weight function) is defined in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Crossing number (graph drawing)

anchor.snippet: The crossing number of a graph is the minimum number of intersections between edges that a drawing of the graph in the plane must contain

annotations.note: Crossing numbers require planar drawings, which are not in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Edge list

anchor.snippet: List structures include the edge list , an array of pairs of vertices

annotations.note: Edge-list data structures are not represented in Mathlib (graphs use adjacency relations).

annotations.label: Adjacency list

anchor.snippet: the adjacency list , which separately lists the neighbors of each vertex

annotations.note: Adjacency-list data structures are not formalized (Mathlib uses neighborFinset, not a list representation).

annotations.label: Incidence matrix

anchor.snippet: the incidence matrix , a matrix of 0's and 1's whose rows represent vertices and whose columns represent edges

annotations.note: The incidence matrix is formalized as SimpleGraph.incMatrix.

annotations.label: Adjacency matrix

anchor.snippet: the adjacency matrix , in which both the rows and columns are indexed by vertices

annotations.note: The adjacency matrix is formalized as SimpleGraph.adjMatrix.

annotations.label: Degree matrix

anchor.snippet: The degree matrix indicates the degree of vertices

annotations.note: The degree matrix is formalized as the diagonal SimpleGraph.degMatrix.

annotations.label: Laplacian matrix

anchor.snippet: The Laplacian matrix is a modified form of the adjacency matrix that incorporates information about the degrees of the vertices

annotations.note: The Laplacian is formalized as SimpleGraph.lapMatrix = degMatrix - adjMatrix.

annotations.label: Kirchhoff's theorem

anchor.snippet: Kirchhoff's theorem on the number of spanning trees of a graph

annotations.note: The Laplacian and a connected-component kernel result exist, but the matrix-tree spanning-tree count theorem is not proved.

annotations.label: Distance matrix

anchor.snippet: The distance matrix , like the adjacency matrix, has both its rows and columns indexed by vertices

annotations.note: Graph distance SimpleGraph.dist is defined, but it is not assembled into a distance matrix.

annotations.label: Subgraph isomorphism problem

anchor.snippet: A common problem, called the subgraph isomorphism problem , is finding a fixed graph as a subgraph in a given graph

annotations.note: The containment relation H ⊑ G (SimpleGraph.IsContained) captures subgraph-isomorphism, but not the decision problem.

annotations.label: Clique problem

anchor.snippet: Finding the largest complete subgraph is called the clique problem

annotations.note: Cliques and the clique number (max clique size) are defined, but not the computational clique problem.

annotations.label: Graph isomorphism problem

anchor.snippet: One special case of subgraph isomorphism is the graph isomorphism problem

annotations.note: Graph isomorphisms (SimpleGraph.Iso, ≃g) are defined, but the decision problem is not addressed.

annotations.label: Independent set problem

anchor.snippet: Finding the largest edgeless induced subgraph or independent set is called the independent set problem

annotations.note: Independent sets (IsIndepSet) and the independence number are defined, but not the computational problem.

annotations.label: Minor

anchor.snippet: A minor or subcontraction of a graph is any graph obtained by taking a subgraph and contracting some (or no) edges

annotations.note: Edge contraction and the minor relation are not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Wagner's Theorem

anchor.snippet: Wagner's Theorem states: A graph is planar if it contains as a minor neither the complete bipartite graph K 3,3

annotations.note: Planarity and the K5/K3,3 forbidden-minor characterization are not in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Subdivision

anchor.snippet: A subdivision or homeomorphism of a graph is any graph obtained by subdividing some (or no) edges

annotations.note: Edge subdivision / topological minors are not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Kuratowski's Theorem

anchor.snippet: Kuratowski's Theorem states: A graph is planar if it contains as a subdivision neither the complete bipartite graph K 3,3

annotations.note: Kuratowski's planarity characterization is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Kelmans–Seymour conjecture

anchor.snippet: the Kelmans–Seymour conjecture : Every 5-vertex-connected graph that is not planar contains a subdivision of the 5-vertex complete graph

annotations.note: The Kelmans–Seymour conjecture is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Reconstruction conjecture

anchor.snippet: The reconstruction conjecture

annotations.note: The reconstruction conjecture is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Strong perfect graph theorem

anchor.snippet: Strong perfect graph theorem

annotations.note: Perfect graphs and the strong perfect graph theorem are not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Graph unification

anchor.snippet: The unification of two argument graphs is defined as the most general graph

annotations.note: Graph subsumption/unification (from term-graph logic) is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Max flow min cut theorem

anchor.snippet: Max flow min cut theorem

annotations.note: Network flows and the max-flow min-cut theorem are not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Dominating set problem

anchor.snippet: Dominating set problem is the special case of set cover problem where sets are the closed neighborhoods

annotations.note: Dominating sets are not defined in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Vertex cover problem

anchor.snippet: Vertex cover problem is the special case of set cover problem where sets to cover are every edges

annotations.note: Vertex covers (IsVertexCover) and the vertex cover number are defined, but not the computational problem.

annotations.label: Set cover problem

anchor.snippet: The original set cover problem, also called hitting set, can be described as a vertex cover in a hypergraph

annotations.note: The set cover / hitting set problem is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Decomposition

anchor.snippet: Decomposition, defined as partitioning the edge set of a graph

annotations.note: Graph decomposition (edge-set partitioning into subgraphs) is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Arboricity

anchor.snippet: Arboricity , a decomposition into as few forests as possible

annotations.note: Arboricity is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Cycle double cover

anchor.snippet: Cycle double cover , a collection of cycles covering each edge exactly twice

annotations.note: Cycle double covers are not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Edge coloring

anchor.snippet: Edge coloring , a decomposition into as few matchings as possible

annotations.note: EdgeLabeling assigns labels to edges, but proper edge coloring (chromatic index / decomposition into matchings) is not defined.

annotations.label: Graph factorization

anchor.snippet: Graph factorization , a decomposition of a regular graph into regular subgraphs of given degrees

annotations.note: Graph factorization (e.g., 1-factorization) is not formalized in Mathlib.
